title: Charmed Life - Chapter 3 (Fragment 3)
text:
“Er—may I go down and have some tea?” he stammered.

“Please yourself,” she said—rather coldly, Cat thought. He ran downstairs again, feeling he might have gotten off on the wrong foot with her.

Gwendolen’s trunk was standing in the middle of her room. Gwendolen herself was sitting in a very queenly way at a round table by the window, with a big pewter teapot in front of her, a plate of brown bread and butter, and a plate of biscuits.

“I told the girl I’d unpack for myself,” she said. “I’ve got secrets in my trunk and my box. And I asked her to bring tea at once because I’m starving. And just look at it! Did you ever see anything so dull? Not even jam!”

“Perhaps the biscuits are nice,” Cat said hopefully. But they were not, or not particularly.

“We shall starve, in the midst of luxury!” sighed Gwendolen.

Her room was certainly luxurious. The wallpaper seemed to be made of blue velvet. The top and bottom of the bed was upholstered like a chair, in blue velvet with buttons in it, and the blue velvet bedspread matched it exactly. The chairs were painted gold. There was a dressing table fit for a princess, with little golden drawers, gold-backed brushes, and a long oval mirror surrounded by a gilded wreath. Gwendolen admitted that she liked the dressing table, though she was not so sure about the wardrobe, which had painted garlands and maypole dancers on it.

“It’s to hang clothes in, not to look at,” she said. “It distracts me. But the bathroom is lovely.”

The bathroom was tiled with blue and white tiles, and the bath was sunk down into the tiled floor. Over it, draped like a baby’s cradle, were blue curtains for when you wanted a shower. The towels matched the tiles. Cat preferred his own bathroom, but that may have been because he had to spend rather a long time in Gwendolen’s. Gwendolen locked him in it while she unpacked. Through the hiss of the shower—Gwendolen had only herself to blame that she found her bathroom thoroughly soaked afterwards—Cat heard her voice raised in annoyance at someone who had come in to take the dull tea away and caught her with her trunk open. When Gwendolen finally unlocked the bathroom door, she was still angry.

“I don’t think the servants here are very civil,” she said. “If that girl says one thing more, she’ll find herself with a boil on her nose—even if her name is Euphemia! Though,” Gwendolen added charitably, “I’m inclined to think being called Euphemia is punishment enough for anyone. You have to go and get your new suit on, Cat. She says dinner’s in half an hour and we have to change for it. Did you ever hear anything so formal and unnatural!”

“I thought you were looking forward to that kind of thing,” said Cat, who most certainly was not.

“You can be grand and natural,” Gwendolen retorted. But the thought of the coming grandeur soothed her all the same. “I shall wear my blue dress with the lace collar,” she said. “And I do think being called Euphemia is a heavy enough burden for anyone to bear, however rude they are.”

As Cat went up his winding stair, the Castle filled with a mysterious booming. It was the first noise he had heard. It alarmed him. He learned later that it was the dressing gong, to warn the Family that they had half an hour to change in. Cat, of course, did not take nearly that time to put his suit on. So he had yet another shower. He felt damp and weak and almost washed out of existence by the time the maid who was so unfortunate in being called Euphemia came to take him and Gwendolen downstairs to the drawing room where the Family was waiting.

vocabulary.definition: A feeling of being irritated or bothered by someone or something.
vocabulary.example: The constant noise was a real annoyance.

vocabulary.definition: A painful, pus-filled bump under the skin caused by infected hair follicles.
vocabulary.example: He had a painful boil on his arm.

vocabulary.definition: Making a deep, resonant sound.
vocabulary.example: The booming voice echoed through the hall.

vocabulary.definition: Courteous and polite behaviour towards others.
vocabulary.example: They tried to be civil despite the disagreement.

ru.translation: сделать что-то неправильно с самого начала

vocabulary.definition: To start badly or be mistaken in initial approach.
vocabulary.example: He got off on the wrong foot with his new boss.

vocabulary.definition: Cover or hang with cloth loosely or casually.
vocabulary.example: The windows were draped with heavy curtains.

vocabulary.definition: A gong sounded to signal that it is time to dress or get ready, especially before a meal or event.
vocabulary.example: The dressing gong sounded, signaling time to prepare.

vocabulary.definition: A piece of furniture with a mirror where one applies makeup or arranges hair.
vocabulary.example: She sat at her dressing table every morning.

vocabulary.definition: Lacking interest or excitement; boring or lacking brightness or vividness.
vocabulary.example: The lecture was dull and unengaging.

vocabulary.definition: Strings or chains of flowers, leaves, or other material, worn or hung as decoration.
vocabulary.example: They decorated the hall with flower garlands.

vocabulary.definition: A decorative ring made of or covered with gold leaf or gold paint.
vocabulary.example: The portrait featured a gilded wreath frame.

vocabulary.definition: Splendour and impressiveness, especially of appearance or style.
vocabulary.example: The palace impressed with its grandeur.

vocabulary.definition: Likely or willing to do something.
vocabulary.example: She was inclined to accept their offer.

vocabulary.definition: A state of great comfort or elegance, especially when involving great expense.
vocabulary.example: They lived in luxury and comfort.

vocabulary.definition: People performing a traditional folk dance around a tall pole decorated with ribbons, typically in spring festivals.
vocabulary.example: At the festival, maypole dancers performed lively dances.

vocabulary.definition: A dull grey alloy of tin with copper and antimony, used for various household items.
vocabulary.example: The antique pewter dishes shone softly in the candlelight.

vocabulary.definition: The infliction or imposition of a penalty as retribution for an offence.
vocabulary.example: The punishment for theft can be severe.

vocabulary.definition: Having the manner, dignity, or appearance of a queen; majestic.
vocabulary.example: She carried herself in a queenly fashion.

vocabulary.definition: Responded sharply or angrily.
vocabulary.example: ‘I don’t agree,’ he retorted quickly.

vocabulary.definition: Extremely wet.
vocabulary.example: His clothes were soaked through after the rain.

vocabulary.definition: Spoke with sudden involuntary pauses and a tendency to repeat the initial letters of words.
vocabulary.example: He stammered when asked a difficult question.

vocabulary.definition: Extremely hungry.
vocabulary.example: After the long journey, I felt starving.

vocabulary.definition: Having bad luck or experiencing misfortune.
vocabulary.example: It was unfortunate that the weather spoiled the picnic.

vocabulary.definition: Furnished with padding, springs, webbing, and fabric or leather covers.
vocabulary.example: The sofa is upholstered in red velvet.

vocabulary.definition: A closely woven fabric of silk, cotton, or nylon, with a thick short pile on one side.
vocabulary.example: She wore a dress made of rich velvet.
